coredata.dc:title: Novel antifouling and antibacterial polyethersulfone membrane prepared by embedding nitrogen-doped carbon dots for efficient salt and dye rejection
coredata.dc:creator: Koulivand, Habib
Shahbazi, Afsaneh
Vatanpour, Vahid
Rahmandoost, Moones
coredata.dc:description: Novel antifouling and antibacterial nanofiltration membranes were prepared by addition of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to the polyethersulfone (PES). The antibacterial NCDs were successfully fabricated using hydrothermal technique and then were characterized using photoluminescence (PL) spectra, FTIR, XRD, and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The resulted nanoparticles were introduced to PES through the phase separation method. The effect of adding NCDs into the PES membrane, as a novel nanofiller was studied in terms of surface and cross-sectional morphology, hydrophilicity, porosity, permeation, fouling resistance, antibacterial properties, and nanofiltration performance. All the NCD-blended membranes exhibited better performance compared to the bare PES. The water flux was significantly increased from 16.5 kg/m2h for the bare PES to 44.6 kg/m2h for the 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane. The 0.50 wt% of NCD-blended PES membrane also showed the best antifouling properties, with a flux recovery ratio (FRR) of 73.1%. The retention sequence of the salts was Na2SO4 (80.3%) > MgSO4 (63.5%) > NaCl (20.7%), showing the common behavior of the negative charge nanofiltration membranes. The antibacterial assessment showed a zone of inhibition for both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria in disks membranes containing higher than 0.10 wt% of NCD concentrations. The results offer NCD-blended membranes as a high potential hydrophilic and antibacterial nanofillers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Carbon dots
Antifouling
Antibacterial
Salt rejection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the Kharazmi University and the Shahid Beheshti University , Iran who provided financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum (a) and XRD pattern (b) of NCDs.
DLS particle size distribution diagram (a) and UV–visible adsorption and PL emission spectra (b) of NCDs.
FE-SEM images of the prepared NF membranes, showing the surface and cross-sectional views.
Water contact angle in the unfilled and NCD-embedded NF membranes (six times of repetition).
Pure water flux of the prepared bare and NCD-embedded nanofiltration membranes (four times repetition).
The permeate fluxes vs. time for the prepared bare and NCD-blended NF membranes during pure water (60 min), foulant (90 min), and second water flux (60 min).
Flux recovery ratio of the prepared bare and NCD-blended NF membranes (four times of repetition).
Fouling parameters of the bare and NCD-blended NF membranes.
Rejection performance of the 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane for Na2SO4, MgSO4 and NaCl, after 90 min filtration (3 bar, 200 mg/L, pH = 7.0 ± 0.1).
Retention behavior of the bare and NCD-blended membrane for Na2SO4, as a function of time (3 bar, 200 mg/L, pH = 7.0 ± 0.1).
Dye rejection results of the NCD-blended NF membranes (four times of repetition).
Inhibitory effects of NCD-blended NF membranes against E. coli (top) and S. aureus (bottom), after 24 h of incubation.
Overall porosity and mean pore radius of the nanocomposite NF membranes.
Comparison of the performance of carbon dots blended PES membranes with and without nitrogen doping.

row.entry: Improvement compared to the bare PES (%)

head.ce:title: Novel antifouling and antibacterial polyethersulfone membrane prepared by embedding nitrogen-doped carbon dots for efficient salt and dye rejection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran 1983969411, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Science Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran 1983969411, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran 15719-14911, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran 1983969411, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: V. Vatanpour, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran 15719-14911, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel antifouling and antibacterial nanofiltration membranes were prepared by addition of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to the polyethersulfone (PES). The antibacterial NCDs were successfully fabricated using hydrothermal technique and then were characterized using photoluminescence (PL) spectra, FTIR, XRD, and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The resulted nanoparticles were introduced to PES through the phase separation method. The effect of adding NCDs into the PES membrane, as a novel nanofiller was studied in terms of surface and cross-sectional morphology, hydrophilicity, porosity, permeation, fouling resistance, antibacterial properties, and nanofiltration performance. All the NCD-blended membranes exhibited better performance compared to the bare PES. The water flux was significantly increased from 16.5 kg/m2h for the bare PES to 44.6 kg/m2h for the 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane. The 0.50 wt% of NCD-blended PES membrane also showed the best antifouling properties, with a flux recovery ratio (FRR) of 73.1%. The retention sequence of the salts was Na2SO4 (80.3%) > MgSO4 (63.5%) > NaCl (20.7%), showing the common behavior of the negative charge nanofiltration membranes. The antibacterial assessment showed a zone of inhibition for both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria in disks membranes containing higher than 0.10 wt% of NCD concentrations. The results offer NCD-blended membranes as a high potential hydrophilic and antibacterial nanofillers.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane technology as a low cost, easy operation, insignificant chemical consumption, and high-efficiency water purification technique has attracted great attention compared to the conventional methods [1]. Membrane separation processes driven by pressure are microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), pervaporation (PV), and reverse osmosis (RO) [2]. Nanofiltration is, in fact, a membrane separation technology with properties (pore size, flux, and operating pressure) between the UF and RO techniques [3]. High rejection performance, relatively high permeation flux, and energy-saving are the main privileges of NF membranes [1]. High retention of divalent ions (Na2SO4 and MgSO4) and low retention of monovalent ions (NaCl) are of the known separation behaviors of the NF membranes [4]. The NF method is extensively used for desalination, wastewater treatment, and water softening [4]. However, flux diminution resulted from organic fouling is the main restriction of these membranes that hinders their large-scale application. Limiting the membrane lifetime, increased operating pressure requirement, feed water loss, altering membrane selectivity, degradation of the membrane, and more chemical cleaning requirement are some issues resulted by membrane fouling [5–7]. Increasing membrane hydrophilicity is a simple solution to deal with fouling phenomenon because most of the fouling agents are hydrophobic in nature [8]. Among various approaches such as UV irradiation, chemical modification, and plasma treatments for the improving of hydrophilicity in the polymeric membranes, blending with hydrophilic nanomaterials has shown to be promising one, due to its convenient and ease operational condition [8,9]. Various hydrophilic materials including carbon-based nanomaterials [6,10–12], metals [6], metal oxides [13–15], silica [16,17], boehmite [18], clay [19], etc. have been introduced to various membranes to enhance the hydrophilicity of the hydrophobic polymers.

Presence of hydrophilic functional groups besides their high surface area, chemical stability, nontoxicity, and well dispersion in organic solvents, makes carbon-based nanomaterials highly effective nanofillers compared to the other nanomaterials [8,20]. Zinadini et al. [11] incorporated graphene oxide (GO) nanosheet into the polyethersulfone (PES) matrix to achieve a membrane with higher flux and antifouling properties. They used the phase inversion technique to mix GO with the PES casting solution and investigate the effect of GO incorporation on the permeability, dye rejection and antifouling performance of the PES membrane. The results presented that all membrane performances and properties including hydrophilicity, water flux, antifouling and dye rejection of the modified PES membrane were improved. Choi et al. [21] modified PSF NF membrane with multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). They used phase inversion technique to mix PSF membrane with different concentrations of MWCNTs, which showed significant improvement in terms of hydrophilicity, permeability and antifouling properties.

On the other hand, biofouling or biological fouling, i.e. attaching, growing and accumulation of microorganisms, especially bacteria, on the wetted surface of membranes, is another type of fouling, which limits membrane application [6,7]. Hence, development of antibacterial membranes has been a common strategy to address this issue. For instance, TiO2 and Ag were two common nanomaterials that have been incorporated into the membrane matrix as antibacterial agents. Kwak and Kim [22] have studied the incorporation of TiO2 nanoparticles into the active layer of polyamide RO membrane to obtain an antibacterial membrane. The resultant membrane showed hydrophilicity improvement and strong antibacterial activity. Vatanpour et al. [6] developed an antifouling and antibacterial PES membrane by addition of Ag-loaded graphene oxide (GO/Ag) to the membrane matrix. Improvement in antibacterial properties of the membrane was proved by observing an increase in the inhibition zone of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new family of quantum dots and a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, have attracted great attention in various fields including photoelectronics, biosensing, drug delivery, photocatalysts, and photovoltaic devices [23,24]. The CDs can be easily synthesized using various carbon sources, such as citric acid [25], glucose [26], ascorbic acid [27], sodium citrate [28], ammonium citrate [29], urea [25], folic acid [30], etc. Application of CDs in membrane technology has recently attracted extensive attention. Several properties such as existence of hydrophilic surface functional groups [23], facile and inexpensive production and functionalization [23,31], well dispersion in organic solvents, ultra-fine size, carbonic core, and nontoxicity [32], introduces CDs as a high-potential candidate for membrane modification.

Li et al. [32] investigated the effect of incorporating different quantities of superhydrophilic quantum-sized CDs on selective layers of polyamide RO membranes through interfacial polymerization. The resultant membranes demonstrated enhancement in hydrophilicity, permeability, and porosity. The 0.02 wt% CD-containing membranes showed the best separation performance and permeability with the salt rejection of 98.8% and pure water flux of 87.1 L/m2h. He et al. [33] integrated Na+-functionalized CDs (Na-CDs) to a polyamide thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane for arsenic and selenium separation. Pyrolysis of citric acid was employed to synthesize ultrafine sized Na-CDs, which were then introduced to the thin layer of the TFN membrane. The Na-CDs modified membranes showed higher permeability and toxic ions rejection, due to decreased pore size and enhanced hydrophilicity, compared to the unfilled membrane. The 0.05 wt% Na-CD-containing membrane showed to have the highest ion rejection (97.5%, 98.2%, and 99.5% for SeO3 2−, SeO4 2−, and HAsO4 2−, respectively), permeability (10.4 L/m2hbar), and fouling resistance.

Bactericide properties of CDs have been reported in various studies [34]. The interaction between the dot nanoparticles and bacteria is the main operational principle behind their microbial activity [34,35]. Since the antibacterial activity of the CDs depends on their surface charge and particles size, surface functionalization can control the process [36]. Yang et al. [37] developed an antibacterial type of quaternized carbon dots using glycerol and dimethyloctaldecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propy] ammonium chloride as reagents. The introduction of the long alkyl chain-containing quaternary ammonium compound endowed the prepared carbon dots with the capability to interact with the negatively charged bacterial cells via electrostatic interaction. The results demonstrated that adsorption of CDs causes destruction of bacterial cell surface since the CDs change the charge balance of the bacterial surface and can insert into the bacterial surface via the long alkyl chain, which leads to the inactivation of Gram-positive bacteria. The application of the above mentioned quaternized CDs was also reported by Ran et al. [38] in imaging and eliminating bacterial biofilms.

Among various types of carbon dots with various surface structures, N-doped carbon dots which are favorable for a wide range of applications, due to their higher stability, biocompatibility and abundant functional groups on the surface, were shown to have strong antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli [39]. Comparing the antibacterial activity of sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) versus Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, Travlou et al. [34] demonstrated that NCDs revealed much higher antibacterial activity.

There is still a significant lack of experimental studies in the literature assessing the performance and properties of the PES membrane with CDs. In addition, there are no comprehensive studies to date that evaluate the effect of CDs on antibacterial membrane structure. Hence, the current study aims to evaluate antifouling and antibacterial performance of PES membrane when NCDs is added. NCDs produced through hydrothermal approach were integrated into the PES membrane using the phase inversion. The prepared PES membrane's properties such as morphology, pure water flux, hydrophilicity, antifouling and antibacterial characteristic, and salt and dye separation were then investigated. Both a Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and a Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria were utilized in antibacterial activity evaluation. A typical organic foulant, bovine serum albumin (BSA), was used in assessment of antifouling properties. For nanofiltration assessment, salts (Na2SO4, MgSO4, and NaCl) and dye (Reactive Red 198) rejection were evaluated.

Polyethersulfone (PES Ultrason E6020P, Mw: 58,000 g/mol) was supplied by BASF Company (Germany). Ammonium citrate dibasic was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Reactive red 198 (RR198), dimethylacetamide (DMAc), Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, Mw: 29,000 g/mol), bovine serum albumin (BSA, Mw: 67,000 g/mol), sodium sulfate, magnesium sulfate, and sodium chloride were provided by Merck Company (Germany).

In this method, 2 g of ammonium citrate dibasic was dissolved in double distilled water at a volume of 75 mL to obtain a clear solution. Based on literature [40–43], ammonium citrate can support both the carbon and nitrogen source to synthesize NCDs. It is also considered as a low cost and environmental friendly raw material. The solution was then moved into a Teflon-lined stainless still reactor (150 mL) and was heated for about 18 h in the oven at 150 °C [29]. The resulted solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature, providing a bright yellow solution that indicated the formation of CDs. The solution was centrifuged (12,000 rpm for 15 min) to separate the large particles and then it was filtered using 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the pure NCDs. To provide NCDs powder, the final solution was freeze-dried.

All membranes including the bare and NCD-blended ones were fabricated by the phase inversion technique. The casting solutions contained 21 wt% PES and 1 wt% PVP, which were mixed in DMAc solvent, after addition of different dosages of NCDs nanoparticles (0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.50 wt%, and 1.00 wt%) relative to the PES weight. Firstly, the PVP was added to the DMAc as a pore former. To obtain a well-dispersed solution, adequate amounts of NCDs were added to the solutions and sonicated for 30 min. The PES was then carefully dissolved in the casting solutions under stirring condition and the resultant solutions were stirred for 18 h to obtain homogenous solutions. To eliminate air bubbles, the solutions were degassed in an oven for 3 h at 50 °C. In the next step, 150 μm-thick thin films of the casting were cast on a clean and smooth glass, by a Dr. blade. Finally, the resulted films were placed in deionized water (non-solvent bath) to coagulate and formation of asymmetric nanocomposite membranes. After preliminary coagulation, the membranes were transferred to fresh deionized water and kept for 24 h to achieve ideal phase inversion. Then, they were dried at room temperature.

FTIR analysis (Bruker Optics, Germany) was used to analyse the chemical bonds of the synthesized NCDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of the NCDs was recorded by DMAX-2500 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan), using a Cu Kα1 X-ray radiation source, in the range of 2θ between 1 and 80°, to provide information about the crystalline structure of NCDs. The average particle size and zeta-potential of the NCDs were confirmed by the dynamic light-scattering method (DLS), (Malvern Instrument, MAL1001767, UK). All experiments were conducted in room temperature at the scattering angle of 90°, using 632.8 nm HeNe laser light source, by 10 mW maximum intensity. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy was applied to characterize the optical properties of the NCDs (PerkinElmer's LAMBDA 950 UV/Vis/NIR Spectrophotometer, USA) in optical glass cuvettes, path length 10 mm. Photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent, USA) was used. The samples were diluted with a 1/20 ratio to minimize the re-absorbance effect as much as possible, for the study of the optical properties [44].

MIRA3 TESCAN field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Czech Republic) was applied to characterize cross-sectional morphology and the surface of the prepared membranes. To prepare the membranes for cross-sectional FE-SEM imaging, small pieces were cut out from the membranes and dipped into liquid nitrogen for a few seconds. Then, the frozen membranes were broken using curved forceps. All the membranes were coated by gold layers to obtain conductive samples.

Contact angle measurement technique was employed to determine the surface hydrophilicity of the fabricated membranes, using goniometer (G10 KRUSS, Germany). The contact angle measurement was performed for at least six repetitions for each membrane from different places to avoid experimental error and increase precision.

A gravimetric-based technique was used to determine the overall porosity (ε) of the membranes, by Eq. (1) [8],
(1)
ε =
ω 1
−
ω 2
A × l ×
d w

In this equation, ω
1 and ω
2 are the wet and the dry weights of the membranes (in g), respectively. A presents the surface area of membrane (in cm2), and d w and l are the water density (0.998 g/cm3) and the thickness of membrane (in cm), respectively. Based on the membranes obtained pure water flux and porosity data, Guerout-Elford-Ferry equation (Eq. (2)) was used to estimate mean pore radius (r m) [8],
(2)
r m
=
2.9 − 1.75 ε
× 8 ηlQ
ε × A × ∆ P
where Q is the volume flow of the membranes (m3/s), ΔP is the applied pressure (0.3 MPa) and η is the water viscosity (8.9 × 10−4 Pa·s).

A 200 mL stirred dead-end cell with a membrane area of 19.6 cm2 was employed to investigate the pure water flux, antifouling, and separation performances of the fabricated NF membranes. All experiments were performed at 400 rpm stirring rate to reduce the concentration polarization. The membranes were submerged in deionized water for 12 h prior to use.

For obtaining the pure water flux, the stirred cells were first filled with deionized water and the applied pressure was set to 4 bar. The membranes were compact at this pressure for 30 min and next, the pressure was decreased to 3 bar and the permeated water was collected and weighted for 60 min at room temperature. The pure water flux (J
w,1, kg/m2h) was estimated by [24]:
(3)
J
w ‚ 1
=
M
A . ∆ t
,
where M is the mass of the permeated water (kg) at the time period of Δt (min) and A is the membrane's effective area (m2). This procedure was done in four repetitions times and the average flux was reported.

In the study of the antifouling performance, the stirred cells were first filled with the BSA solution (250 mg/L) and the protein flux (J p, kg/m2h) was recorded at an operating pressure of 3 bar for 90 min. The fouled membranes were then washed and submerged in deionized water for 30 min. After that, the membranes were again placed in the cell and the alternative water flux (J
w,2, kg/m2h) was obtained through the same performed procedure as for J
w,1. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) shows the antifouling capability of the membranes; in a way that the higher the FRR is, the better the antifouling properties of the membrane would be. The FRR of the bare and NCD-blended membranes was calculated using Eq. (4) [24]:
(4)
FRR
%
=
J
w ‚ 2
J
w ‚ 1
× 100 .

In order to investigate the fouling phenomenon more detailed, several fouling ratios were estimated, including reversible (R r), irreversible (R ir) and total (R t) fouling ratios. The total fouling is calculated as follows [24]:
(5)
R t
=
1 −
J p
J
w ‚ 1
× 100 .

The irreversible and reversible fouling ratios, as to be the fouling resulted by stable deposition or adsorption of the organic substances causing pore block and flux decline, and the fouling caused by the concentration polarization of foulants on the surface of the membrane [8,13], respectively, were calculated using Eqs. (6) and (7), respectively [24]:
(6)
R ir
=
J
w ‚ 1
−
J
w ‚ 2
J
w ‚ 1
× 100 ,
(7)
R r
=
J
w ‚ 2
−
J p
J
w ‚ 1
× 100 .

Nanofiltration performance of the mixed matrix membranes was tested by filtering 200 mg/L solutions of monovalent (NaCl) and divalent salts (Na2SO4 and MgSO4) for 90 min, followed by the filtration of a 100 mg/L solution of anionic dye RR198 at pH = 7.0 ± 0.1 at room temperature, for 60 min, under an operating pressure of 3 bar. The conductivity meter was used to measure the concentration of the single salts in the permeate water and the concentration of the RR198 dye in the permeate and the feed solution was obtained by a Hach spectrophotometer (DR 2800, Germany) at λmax of 518 nm. Eq. (8) was used to calculate the concentrations of RR198 and salts,
(8)
R
%
=
1 −
C
C 0
× 100
where C and C o represents the measured concentration/conductivity of the RR198/salts in the permeate and the feed solution (mg/L), respectively.

The antibacterial effect of the addition of NCDs to the membranes was assessed using the disk diffusion method. The visual observation of the inhibition zone was considered as an indication of the antibacterial performance of the NCD containing membranes and all tests were performed in triplicate. An autoclave was used to sterilize all plates and supplies before running the experiments and the samples were exposed to the two bacteria species, namely E. coli ATCC25922 and S. aureus ATCC25923 (American Type Culture Collection, Rockville, MD, USA).

For the purpose of testing, bacteria suspensions were inoculated overnight at about 100 cfu/mL. Using swabs, the suspensions were spread onto the Mueller Hinton agar (Sigma Aldrich Chemie GmbH Co., Taufkirchen, Germany), using the lawn culture technique. Two circular disks of each membrane, measuring 6 mm in diameter, were placed on the nutrient plates from their top side. Water and gentamicin were used as negative and positive controls, respectively. The inoculated Petri dishes were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the inhibition zones were photographed using Samsung galaxy S7 smartphone [6,45].

The characteristic chemical bonds' absorption patterns in the NCDs were obtained, as provided in Fig. 1a. The FTIR spectrum of the NCDs confirmed the presence of a number of functional groups in a nanoparticle structure, like carboxylic and amine functional groups, with vibrations ranging from 1040 to 1060 cm−1 and around 3485 to 3560 cm−1, respectively. Amide bands between 1600 and 1700 cm−1 and aromatic CC band at about 1450 cm−1 are the other two significantly observed chemical bonds [46–48].

Fig. 1b illustrates the XRD diagram of the as-prepared NCDs, which shows the characteristic (002) and (100) graphitic carbon crystalline phases. The broad peak width in the diffraction pattern is due to the small size of the NCDs [48].

As illustrated in Fig. 2a, in the study of the average particle size and size distribution of the NCDs, DLS technique was employed. Consequently, the diameters are shown to be mainly in the range of 22 to 31 nm, with an average diameter of 25.8 nm. The narrow peak in the DLS report admits the rather uniform distribution of NCDs size.

In addition, the surface charge of particles (so-called zeta potential) was measured under an electric field using the same instrument in three replications. The zeta-potential results indicated that NCDs have high negative charges (−16.7 ± 0.6 mV) on their surface. It should be noted that the negative charge of the NCDs is attributed to the existence of amine, amide and carboxyl groups on their surface [48].

In this study, as shown in Fig. 2b, the obtained UV–visible spectrum of the NCDs, show a maximal absorption at the wavelength of 355 nm. Under an excitation wavelength of 355 nm, NCDs show an emission wavelength of 450 nm.

FE-SEM images were used to investigate the possible morphology changes on the surface and cross-sectional area of the fabricated NF membranes. The images of the prepared membranes with various concentrations of NCDs are illustrated in Fig. 3 . Comparison of the surface FE-SEM images shows smooth and relatively similar surface morphology in the bare and NCD-blended PES membranes, showing no surface cracks. Furthermore, the aggregation of NCDs nanoparticles was not observed, making it possible to conclude that the stability and mechanical characteristics of the NCD-blended membranes have not been influenced by the addition of NCD nanoparticles [49,50].

The cross-sectional views of the unfilled and NCD-embedded membranes are also presented in Fig. 3, at two different magnifications, showing the common asymmetric structure for all prepared membranes. In addition, a finger-like porous cross-sectional morphology was observed in all membranes with a spacious sub-layer and dense top-layer. However, in NCD-blended membranes, the finger-like channels were observed to be longer, wider with more regular pores and channels, which can enhance the permeation of these membranes [51]. In asymmetric membranes, kinetics and thermodynamics of the phase inversion process are known to be the main influencing factors on the cross-sectional morphology [52]. Hydrophilic NCDs can, therefore, enhance the mass transfer rate during the solvent and non-solvent phase inversion processes, leading to creation of larger pores and channels [49,52].

Table 1 provides the mean pore radius and overall porosity of the nanocomposite NF membranes, showing that the mean pore radius and overall porosity were higher in the NCD-blended membranes. After addition of 0.50 wt% of NCDs, the overall porosity and mean pore radius were increased from 57.0% and 3.48 nm to 69.7% and 4.95 nm, respectively. The increased overall porosity and pore size can be attributed to the migration of hydrophilic NCD nanomaterials to the upper surface of the membrane in the coagulation process which effects the non-solvent and solvent phase separation processes and creates bigger channels during the replacing of DMAc with water [3,53]. Moreover, in higher concentrations of NCDs (1.00 wt%), decreasing in overall porosity and pore size can be explained by aggregation of higher contents of nanoparticles around the pores, which results in shrinkage of the size of the pores and channels [54].

The membranes' surface hydrophilicity, as a key factor affecting fouling properties of the membrane [54], can facilitate the passage of water molecules and improve their permeability. Generally, the surface hydrophilicity can be quantified by measuring the water contact angle, where lower contact angles show more hydrophilic surface properties [51]. As presented in Fig. 4 , by increasing NCDs content in the membrane matrix, the contact angle of the membranes reduced consistently, which can be described by the hydrophilic nature of NCDs. The hydrophilic NCDs migrate to the membrane upper surface in the coagulation process and create a hydrophilic surface. Thus, the highest and lowest contact angles were observed in the bare PES (61.5°) and 1.00 wt% NCDs (44.7°), respectively. Existence of hydrophilic functional groups including amine, amide, and carbonyl on the surface of NCDs, at explained earlier, decrease the water contact angle and improve wettability of the resultant membranes.

Fig. 5 shows the chart of the pure water flux for the bare and NCD-embedded membranes, reporting higher values of water permeability for all NCD-blended membranes. A lot of studies have proved the relationship between hydrophilicity and pure water flux [51]. In this case, as shown earlier, surface functional groups of the NCDs (amide, amine, and carbonyl groups) make them hydrophilic nanoparticles to enhance membrane surface hydrophilicity and wettability and subsequently enhance the permeability of the NCD-blended membranes. The obtained results are in good agreement with the contact angle measurement data and the overall porosity and pore size results.

The pure water flux of the PES membrane reached to 44.6 kg/m2h, after blending 0.50 wt% of NCDs to the casting solution, while the corresponding flux for the bare PES was 16.5 kg/m2h. This increase in the pure water flux can be explained in details from three different aspects: (a) the higher hydrophilicity of the NCD-blended membranes increase the wettability of these membranes compared to the unfilled PES; (b) the higher overall porosity of the blended membranes results in more water content capacity; (c) the solvent-non-solvent exchange can be boosted after addition of the NCDs during the phase inversion which results in increasing water transportation [51]. However, the permeability of the NCD-blended membranes was appeared to decrease after further increase of NCD content from 0.50 wt% to 1.00 wt%, which can be due to the possible aggregation of nanoparticles and lower pore size, as observed in the mean pore size results.

Fouling phenomenon, a major difficulty in the membrane technology, is obstacle for appropriate flux, longevity of the membranes, and increase of the maintenance costs. Therefore, a majority of research studies have focused on improving antifouling properties of the membranes. In the present study, the antifouling properties of the bare and NCD-blended membranes were investigated using BSA as a strong foulant protein. For this purpose, the permeate flux was recorded before, during and after the filtration of BSA, by measuring the pure water flux (1–60 min), BSA flux (61–150 min), and alternative water flux (151–210 min), respectively, as presented in Fig. 6 . A sharp flux decline can be observed during the BSA filtration as a result of the concentration polarization of the fouling agents on the membrane surface. All NCD-blended membranes had a higher permeability compared to the bare PES before, during and after the foulant filtration. As can be observed, both BSA flux and alternative water flux were increased after addition of NCDs and the highest flux was observed for the membrane containing 0.50 wt% of NCDs. The higher flux and better antifouling properties of the blended membranes could be attributed to the more hydrophilicity resulted by the NCDs. When surface hydrophilicity is increased, adsorption of hydrophobic foulant agent on the surface of the membrane and subsequently, total and irreversible fouling is decreased. The presence of N-CDs in the membrane matrix increased the accessible amide, amine, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups on the surface of the CDs blended membranes, facilitating the creation of a thin layer of water film by hydrogen bonds on the PES membrane which prevented the adsorption of BSA molecules from improving the antifouling property of the CDs blended membranes [24].

Fig. 7 presents the results of the FRR, as the most expressive fouling factor performance. Higher FRRs were observed for the membranes with lower contact angle and higher surface hydrophilicity and pure water flux results. This observation can be explained by this fact that a thin layer of water molecules is formed on the surface of the hydrophilic membrane and prevents adsorption of the foulant molecules [11]. Thus, the highest FRR of the 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane can be explained by its superior surface hydrophilicity. The FRR values for the unfilled PES and 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membranes were 52.8% and 73.1%, respectively, which validates good antifouling characteristics of the NCD-embedded membranes.

Moreover, the fouling ratios as to be the R t, R ir, and R r fouling ratios were provided in Fig. 8 . The R t and R ir values showed a decreasing trend by addition of 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, and 0.50 wt% content of the NCD nanoparticles and then decreased after reaching 1.00 wt%, because adsorption of the hydrophobic organic foulants on the surface of membrane is diminished by increasing surface hydrophilicity and pure water flux. The results demonstrated that the bare PES membrane had the highest R t, and R ir fouling ratios (56.3% and 47.2%, respectively) compared to the NCD-blended membranes. In addition, the 0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane indicated the lowest R t, and R ir (37.3% and 26.9%, respectively), due to the higher hydrophilicity. Unlike the R t, and R ir fouling ratios, the R r fouling ratio was increased by the addition of the NCDs. In fact, the fouling molecules were appeared to be dispersed more efficiently from the blended membranes' surface compared to bare membrane. In this case, fouling is generated by the aggregation of the fouling molecules on the surface of the membrane with a unstable connection, which can be easily removed by the simple means of hydraulic cleaning [8,55]. The 0.50 wt% NCDs membrane showed to have the best antifouling properties with the highest FRR of 73.1% and the lowest irreversible fouling ratio of 26.9%.

As shown in Fig. 9 , the retention performance of the optimum membrane (0.50 wt% NCD-blended membrane) was tested against three common monovalent and divalent salts, including NaCl, MgSO4, and Na2SO4, with 200 mg/L feed concentration at solution pH of 7, under the applied pressure of 3 bar at 25 ± 1 °C. The retention of salt was in order of Na2SO4 (80.3%) > MgSO4 (63.5%) > NaCl (20.7%). This is in agreement with potential NF membrane characteristics which shows the lower retention of the monovalent ions and higher retention of divalent ions [56]. Due to the presence of the divalent cation Mg2+ in MgSO4, the rejection performance of this sulfate (divalent anion in MgSO4) is lower than that of Na+ in Na2SO4. The lowest salt rejection was observed for NaCl, which includes two monovalent ions [12].

As presented in Fig. 10 , the rejection of all NCD-blended NF membranes for Na2SO4 was greater than the unfilled PES membrane. The highest rejection capacity was achieved for the membranes having 0.50 wt% and 1.00 wt% of NCDs. This observation can be attributed by the stronger negative charges on the surface of the NCD-embedded membranes in comparison to the pure PES, due to the Donnan effect [57].

Discharging wastewaters containing toxic organic dyes to the surface waters without appropriate treatment can adversely influence aquatic environment. Therefore, it is necessary to remove these pollutants by an efficient treatment technology, before discharging to the recipient streams. Recently, NF technology has been successfully applied for efficient dye wastewater treatment [8].

Filtration of RR198 anionic textile dye in a feed concentration of 100 mg/L, at pH = 7, under 3 bar operating pressure was performed in 60 min, in order to evaluate the separation performance of NF membranes. Fig. 11 illustrates the molecular structure of the RR198 used in this study.

The results of dye rejection percentage by application of all prepared NF membranes are presented in Fig. 12 . The near-total dye rejection in all membranes shows their high NF performance. The dye rejection was, however, higher in all NCD-blended membranes than in the bare one and the membranes containing 0.50 wt% and 1.00 wt% of NCDs exhibited the highest separation performance (99.2%). The repulsion effect created between the anionic dye with negative charge and negatively charged membranes is responsible for the more separation performance of the NCD-embedded membranes. According to the zeta potential results the NCD nanoparticles are negatively charged in neutral condition. RR198 is also known as an anionic dye with negative charge in neutral pH.

Bactericide properties of NCDs have been reported in previous studies [34]. In this study, the antibacterial activity of the membranes was assessed using the disk diffusion method. As shown in Fig. 13 , a zone of inhibition was observed for the membranes with the NCD concentrations higher than 0.10 wt%, while the inhibition zone was not observed for the disk containing bare PES. The inhibition also was not obvious for the concentrations lower than 0.20 wt%. The diameter of the zone of inhibition was increased by increasing NCDs content and the membranes containing 0.50 and 1.00 wt% of N-CDs exhibited the highest antibacterial activity. However, the inhibition effect for both Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria was almost equal. According to literature, various carbon dots are known to have antibacterial properties [58]. This unique property is known to occur based on different mechanisms. The interaction between the dot nanoparticles and the bacteria could be the main operation principle behind their microbial activity [34,35]. Damaging of the membrane of cell is one of the major mechanisms of the antibacterial property in carbon dots. This characteristic is generally observed when the cell wall is influenced by the carbon dot's sharp edges, which leads to leakage of cytoplasm, and influences the genetic process of the microorganism after binding to DNA and RNA. In addition, nonzero Gaussian curvature of some carbon dots is consider to match well with that of the cell surface of some bacteria, e.g. S. aureus, which demonstrates an antimicrobial process [59,60]. On the other hand, enhanced antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli was observed as a result of the existence of hydroxyl radicals (-OH), in NCDs, as the second known mechanism of antibacterial activity in carbon dots.
Eventually, by considering the flux and fouling resistance experiments, 0.50 wt% can be suggested as the most proper concentration of N-CDs in application.

Novel antifouling and antibacterial membranes were prepared by addition of nitrogen-doped carbon dots to the PES membrane polymer through the phase inversion method. The obtained membranes were characterized using FE-SEM and the contact angle and overall porosity of the membranes were analyzed. The effects of the addition of NCD nanoparticles to the PES membrane were investigated in terms of permeability, antifouling and antibacterial properties and rejection performance.

A finger-like and porous cross-sectional structure was observed with a dense top-layer and a spacious sub-layer. Hydrophilicity and porosity of the membranes were both improved by the addition of the NCDs, showing an increased pure water flux and improved antifouling properties. The highest pure water flux and fouling resistance were observed for the 0.50 wt% NCD-embedded membrane. The higher irreversible fouling of the NCD-embedded membranes, compared to that of the unfilled PES, represented the lower protein adsorption on the modified membranes. The resulted membranes also exhibited common characteristic as of the NF membranes with lower retention of the monovalent ions and higher rejection of divalent ions. The antibacterial assessment showed antibacterial properties of the membranes with NCD concentrations higher than 0.10 wt%.

The diameter of the zone of inhibition was increased by increasing NCDs content and the membranes containing 0.50 and 1.00 wt% of N-CDs exhibited the highest antibacterial activity. Eventually, by considering the flux and fouling resistance experiments, 0.50 wt% can be suggested as the most proper concentration of N-CDs in application.

The effect of nitrogen doping can be explored by comparing the results to our previous work [24] performed with pure carbon dots (without nitrogen doping) presented in Table 2 . According to the results, pure water flux of the PES membrane has increased 94.2 and 170.3% after the addition of 0.50 wt% of CDs and N-CDs, respectively, showed better flux enhancement resulted by nitrogen doping. The FRR improvement was relatively higher for the PES membrane modified with pure CDs, while, fouling decline was almost equal for both membranes. Usually, by increase of flux, the FRR reduces. Moreover, both membranes showed same salt rejection performance. Despite all differences between the performances of these two membranes, the main advantage of the N-CDs blended membrane was antibacterial activity.

Habib Koulivand: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation. Afsaneh Shahbazi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources. Vahid Vatanpour: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Moones Rahmandoost: Formal analysis, Resources.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Kharazmi University and the Shahid Beheshti University, Iran who provided financial support.
